title: Named Entity Recognition with BERT
concept: BERT solves NER by predicting an IOB entity tag for every single token position, leveraging bidirectional context for high precision.

exercises.prompt: What does the acronym 'NER' stand for in natural language processing?
exercises.explain: Named Entity Recognition (NER) is all about identifying and labeling specific entities like names of people, organizations, and geographic locations.

cards.title: Spotting Names in a Crowd
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how smart assistants instantly know that 'Meet Alice at Starbucks' means a person named Alice and a place called Starbucks? It's not magic—it's a model like BERT classifying every single word in the blink of an eye!

cards.title: What is NER with BERT?
cards.body: Named Entity Recognition (NER) is the task of finding and labeling key details in text, like names, places, and dates. BERT solves this by analyzing the entire sentence at once and predicting a tag (like 'Person', 'Location', or 'Outside') for every single token.

cards.title: The Highlighter Team
cards.body: Imagine reading a sentence while holding a bunch of highlighter markers. As your eyes move across each word, you immediately highlight people in blue, locations in yellow, and organizations in green. BERT does exactly this: it looks at the entire sentence and holds up a specific 'label marker' for every single word at the same time.

cards.title: Token by Token Tagging
cards.body:
Let's look at the sentence: "Alice visited Paris." 

BERT processes each token and assigns a label:
• "Alice" -> B-PER (Beginning of a Person)
• "visited" -> O (Outside of any entity)
• "Paris" -> B-LOC (Beginning of a Location)

cards.title: Not Just for Sentences
cards.body: Many people think BERT can only classify entire sentences as positive or negative. In reality, BERT has a 'token classification' setup that lets it label every single individual word, making it perfect for finding specific names and locations!

cards.title: One-Way vs. Two-Way Vision
cards.body: Traditional models read text in one direction, often missing the clues that come later in a sentence. BERT reads the whole sentence at once, using both left and right clues to get the labels right.

right.text: Reads left and right at the same time. Instantly knows 'Apple' is a company because it sees 'stock' and 'price' nearby.

left.text: Reads left-to-right. Might mistake 'Apple' for a fruit before reading the word 'stock' later in the sentence.

exercises.prompt: How does BERT analyze a sentence to assign entity tags?

options.text: It only reads text from left to right.
It looks at both left and right context simultaneously.
It translates the text into another language first.
It only reads the very last word of the sentence.

exercises.explain: BERT uses bidirectional context, meaning it looks at the words before and after each token to understand its exact meaning and label.

exercises.prompt: In NER tagging, the 'O' tag stands for 'Outside' of any named entity.
exercises.explain: In standard NER datasets, the 'O' tag stands for 'Outside' and is used for regular words that are not part of any named entity (like verbs or prepositions).

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe how BERT labels text for NER.
exercises.sentence: BERT solves NER by predicting a label for every single ________ in the sentence.
exercises.explain: For Named Entity Recognition, BERT makes a prediction for every individual token (word or sub-word) in the sentence to determine if it is part of a named entity.

exercises.prompt: An AI is training to tag the sentence 'London visited Sarah.' Which set of tags is correct?
exercises.explain: The AI response correctly maps 'London' as a location (B-LOC) and 'Sarah' as a person (B-PER), while the other options mix up the tags.

exercises.prompt: Why is BERT so good at distinguishing between 'Apple' the company and 'apple' the fruit in Named Entity Recognition?

options.text: It reads bidirectionally to understand the surrounding context of each word.
It guesses tags randomly based on the first letter of each word.
It only reads words from right to left, skipping punctuation.
It ignores the surrounding words and only looks at a pre-made dictionary.

exercises.explain: BERT reads the entire sentence in both directions at once. This allows it to easily figure out that 'Apple' refers to the company (an organization) rather than the fruit, because it looks at the surrounding words like 'released' and 'iPhone'.
